title: Vector Database (pgvector)
description: pgvector is a PostgreSQL extension for vector similarity search. Adds vector data type and indexing to standard PostgreSQL. Best for: combining relational data with vector search, keeping vectors alongside existing data, self-hosted deployments. Lower cost than SaaS but requires PostgreSQL ops expertise.

provides.description: Store vectors in PostgreSQL with vector data type
provides.reasoning: pgvector's primary function is storing vector embeddings as a native PostgreSQL data type, making this a core capability with native type system integration.
pgvector enables vector similarity search using distance metrics (L2, cosine, inner product), which is the main use case for vector databases and is highly optimized through specialized indexing.

provides.description: Join vector search with relational data in same database
provides.reasoning: A key differentiator of pgvector is the ability to join vector data with relational tables in the same database, enabling hybrid queries that combine vector similarity with traditional SQL operations.
pgvector supports IVFFlat and HNSW indexes which are critical for performance at scale, enabling efficient approximate nearest neighbor search on large vector datasets.
pgvector runs on PostgreSQL and inherits all standard relational database capabilities including ACID transactions, SQL queries, constraints, and data integrity features.
pgvector runs inside PostgreSQL; RLS policies apply to vector tables just like any other table, enabling per-tenant vector search isolation.
pgvector is a PostgreSQL extension that participates in PostgreSQL transactions including XA/two-phase commit distributed transaction coordination.

requires.reasoning: pgvector is a PostgreSQL extension and cannot function without a PostgreSQL database instance. This is a hard dependency as it extends PostgreSQL's type system and indexing capabilities.

distance_metric.description: Distance operator for similarity search. Cosine for normalized vectors (most common), L2 for Euclidean distance, inner product for dot product similarity.

vector_dimensions.description: Dimensionality of vectors. Common: 1536 (OpenAI ada-002), 768 (sentence-transformers), 384 (MiniLM). Higher dimensions = more storage and slower queries.

index_type.description: Index algorithm. HNSW: faster queries, more memory, better recall. IVFFlat: memory efficient, good for large datasets. None: exact search, no index overhead.

hnsw_m.description: HNSW max connections per layer. Higher = better recall but more memory. Typical: 16. Trade-off between index size and query performance.

hnsw_ef_construction.description: HNSW build-time search depth. Higher = better index quality but slower builds. Typical: 64-200. Affects index creation time vs query accuracy.

ivfflat_lists.description: IVFFlat cluster count. Rule of thumb: sqrt(row_count). More lists = faster queries but slower inserts. Balance between query speed and write performance.

maintenance_work_mem.description: PostgreSQL memory for index creation. Higher = faster index builds. Set based on available RAM. Critical for large vector datasets.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: PostgreSQL hosting with pgvector extension. Min ($15): Self-hosted on small VPS (DigitalOcean Droplet 2GB RAM, 1 vCPU) or AWS RDS db.t3.micro with minimal storage for development/testing with <100K vectors. Max ($2000): Production deployment on AWS RDS db.r6g.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 64GB RAM) with 500GB SSD storage, Multi-AZ setup, and automated backups for millions of vectors with high-performance indexing (HNSW/IVFFlat). Mid-range ($200-500): db.t3.large with 100GB storage handles most medium-scale applications with 1-5M vectors. Note: pgvector itself is free, costs are purely infrastructure (compute + storage + backup).
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity implementation. Requires: (1) PostgreSQL administration knowledge for performance tuning, (2) Understanding vector similarity search algorithms (cosine, L2, inner product), (3) Index selection and optimization (IVFFlat vs HNSW trade-offs), (4) Schema design to integrate vectors with relational data, (5) Query optimization for hybrid searches (filtering + vector similarity), (6) Embedding generation pipeline integration. Learning curve: 1-2 weeks for experienced PostgreSQL developers, 3-4 weeks for those new to vector databases. Implementation: 20-40 hours for setup, indexing strategy, and integration. Lower than specialized vector DBs like Pinecone/Weaviate due to PostgreSQL familiarity, but higher than simple key-value stores.
provenance.licenseCost: pgvector is open source (PostgreSQL License, similar to MIT/BSD), completely free for commercial use with no restrictions. PostgreSQL itself is also open source. No licensing fees, vendor lock-in, or usage-based charges. All costs are infrastructure-only. Commercial support available through PostgreSQL service providers (e.g., EnterpriseDB, Crunchy Data) but optional.

supports_nfr.reason: PostgreSQL with pgvector achieves up to 99.9% availability due to failover complexity with extension dependencies.
Vector similarity search with pgvector requires at least 10ms p95 latency due to index traversal and distance calculations.
Complex vector queries with filtering and joins can require at least 50ms p99 latency under load.
PostgreSQL failover with pgvector extension requires at least 5 minutes for automatic recovery and extension verification.
PostgreSQL with pgvector handles up to approximately 10M vector operations per hour on typical infrastructure.
pgvector read queries including vector similarity search are limited to approximately 1000 QPS due to computational overhead.
pgvector write operations including vector indexing are limited to approximately 500 QPS due to index maintenance overhead.
PostgreSQL supports multiple authentication methods through pg_hba.conf configuration and extensions.
PostgreSQL pgaudit extension provides comprehensive audit logging capabilities that can be enabled or disabled.
PostgreSQL with pgvector supports all tenant isolation strategies through row-level security, schemas, or separate databases.
PostgreSQL encryption and row-level security enable secure handling of PII data in vector embeddings or alongside them.
PostgreSQL deletion capabilities, audit logging, and encryption support GDPR compliance for vector data.
PostgreSQL with encryption, audit logging, and access controls can meet HIPAA requirements for vector embeddings of health data.
PostgreSQL data access controls and deletion features support CCPA compliance for vector search applications.
PostgreSQL transaction logging and audit trails support SOX compliance requirements for financial data with vectors.
PostgreSQL transaction isolation levels guarantee read-your-writes consistency for vector operations within transactions.
PostgreSQL ACID compliance with fsync and synchronous_commit ensures strict durability for vector data when configured.

supports_constraints.reason: pgvector runs on PostgreSQL which can be deployed on any major cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure.
pgvector is accessed via standard PostgreSQL drivers available in all major programming languages.
pgvector database extension can be accessed from any application platform type that connects to PostgreSQL.
pgvector pattern requires vector_search feature to be enabled as it provides vector similarity search capabilities.
pgvector works with or without caching layers for vector similarity results.
pgvector vector storage is compatible with both synchronous and asynchronous application architectures.
pgvector stores vector embeddings from AI models but does not perform inference itself, working with or without inference features.
pgvector supports multi-tenant architectures through PostgreSQL row-level security and schema isolation or single-tenant deployments.
pgvector handles both real-time vector queries and batch vector ingestion workloads equally well.
pgvector participates in PostgreSQL transactions including distributed transactions via two-phase commit when needed.
pgvector can store vectors from real-time streams or serve batch workloads equally well.
PostgreSQL with pgvector can store JSONB documents alongside vectors for hybrid search or operate purely with vectors.
PostgreSQL with pgvector can function as key-value store using primary keys alongside vector search or use other access patterns.
PostgreSQL supports graph queries through extensions like Apache AGE alongside pgvector or without graph capabilities.
PostgreSQL TimescaleDB extension can be combined with pgvector for time-series vector data or used without time-series features.
pgvector handles transactional vector operations within PostgreSQL OLTP workloads or analytical vector queries.
pgvector supports analytical vector queries alongside PostgreSQL OLAP extensions or purely transactional workloads.

warn_nfr.reason: pgvector adds PostgreSQL extension overhead and index maintenance complexity. Below 10 QPS, simpler storage solutions may be more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: db-vector--pgvector: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<10). Vector database overhead may not be justified at this scale; consider simpler storage.
